episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down Tod Maffin and [Williams Lake Tribune] ‘Escaped Alone’ wins best production in Williams Lake, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can rural communities maintain authentic cultural voices and documentation when the infrastructure—connectivity, platforms, and AI tools—is controlled by external corporations? Topics include: Platform-independent publishing and the Tod Maffin/Canadaland models, Rural broadband infrastructure monopolies and the Rogers-Shaw merger enforcement gap, Community-owned broadband alternatives and the Olds, Alberta fiber co-op model, Indigenous cultural documentation and governance through tools like Mukurtu, Funding structures for cultural organizations and the project-versus-infrastructure mismatch.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Cariboo Arts & Culture Society

discussion.central_question: How can rural communities maintain authentic cultural voices and documentation when the infrastructure—connectivity, platforms, and AI tools—is controlled by external corporations?
discussion.riley_position: The question isn't who owns infrastructure, but whether communities can use it to tell true stories about themselves; cultural production tools have become cheap and accessible enough that communities can produce and distribute content independently.
riley_key_evidence: Tod Maffin built 10 million weekly readers across newsletter, podcast, and Bluesky after leaving CBC
Canadaland grew from scratch without CBC alumni backing to create original investigative series and fellowships
Mukurtu is an open-source content management system built specifically for Indigenous cultural archives with community-controlled permissions
discussion.casey_position: Rural cultural organizations face asymmetric accountability risks and resource constraints; the real ceiling isn't tools or willingness but structural funding mismatches that treat infrastructure investments as short-term projects rather than multi-year capacity building.
casey_key_evidence: A 100 Mile House community radio station with two staff and volunteers cannot absorb accountability failures the way larger outlets can
Rogers is behind on rural 5G deployment commitments made at the Shaw merger approval
Arts councils fund projects, not the 3-5 year training pipelines needed to make tools like Mukurtu self-sustaining
discussion.resolution: They agreed to disagree on whether funding gaps are solvable but converged on the core insight: the structural problem is funding being categorized for projects rather than infrastructure, and the real question is designing cultural organizations to be self-sustaining by design rather than refundable on two-year cycles.
topics_covered: Platform-independent publishing and the Tod Maffin/Canadaland models
Rural broadband infrastructure monopolies and the Rogers-Shaw merger enforcement gap
Community-owned broadband alternatives and the Olds, Alberta fiber co-op model
Indigenous cultural documentation and governance through tools like Mukurtu
Funding structures for cultural organizations and the project-versus-infrastructure mismatch
